title: Cumulant dynamics in finite-memory diffusion
authors: Navid Abbasi, Xin An, Shanjin Wu
abstract: Fluctuations of conserved charges are among the main proposed signatures of the quantum chromodynamics (QCD) critical point, but their interpretation requires a dynamical description of how fluctuation correlators evolve during the finite lifetime of the quark--gluon plasma (QGP) fireball. The standard baseline for this evolution is Fickian diffusion, in which the diffusive current follows the local density gradient instantaneously. This instantaneous-current limit can miss delayed-response effects when the current-relaxation time becomes comparable to the relaxation time of the relevant fluctuation modes. In this work we extend this baseline to Maxwell--Cattaneo diffusion, where the current relaxes on a finite time scale and therefore retains memory. We derive closed evolution equations for multi-point Wigner functions and convert the freezeout correlators into acceptance-dependent cumulants along representative trajectories in the QCD phase diagram. While Fickian diffusion already causes the correlators to lag behind their instantaneous equilibrium values, finite current relaxation introduces an additional memory effect beyond this diffusive lag. As a result, current memory can suppress, shift, and reshape the non-monotonic behavior of the cumulants relative to both instantaneous equilibrium and Fickian diffusion, with the most visible effects appearing in higher-order cumulants and their ratios.
summary: This work develops a theoretical framework to describe how charge fluctuations evolve in the rapidly expanding QGP, moving beyond simple Fickian diffusion. By incorporating finite current relaxation time (Maxwell-Cattaneo diffusion), the authors show that memory effects significantly alter the measurable cumulants. This provides a more accurate diagnostic tool for probing the QCD critical point.
main_problem: The paper addresses the complex, non-equilibrium evolution of fluctuation correlators for conserved charges in the Quark-Gluon Plasma (QGP) fireball, especially when current relaxation effects are significant.
main_result: Finite current memory, modeled by Maxwell-Cattaneo diffusion, introduces an additional memory effect beyond standard diffusive lag, which can suppress, shift, and reshape the non-monotonic behavior of higher-order cumulants.
method: The authors derive closed evolution equations for multi-point Wigner functions, extending the standard Fickian diffusion baseline to include finite current relaxation time scales.
model_or_system: The system is the QGP undergoing rapid expansion, analyzed using theoretical frameworks that extend standard hydrodynamics to incorporate memory effects via the Maxwell-Cattaneo constitutive relation.
key_observables: Acceptance-dependent cumulants ($C_N(\Delta)$) of conserved charges, and the evolution of multi-point Wigner functions ($W_N$).
important_parameters: Current relaxation time ($	au$), diffusive lag time, and the proximity to the QCD critical point.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the ability to derive closed deterministic evolution equations for multi-point correlators and compares results against the $	au 	o 0$ (Fickian) limit.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the comparison of cumulant evolution under different diffusion models and potentially show background trajectories in the QCD phase diagram.
paper_structure: The paper builds from establishing the link between cumulants and correlators, deriving the Maxwell-Cattaneo evolution equations for the correlators, and finally numerically comparing the resulting cumulant dynamics against the standard Fickian baseline.
why_interesting: The rigorous mathematical treatment of memory effects in transport coefficients, leading to modified evolution equations for correlation functions, provides a generalizable framework for non-equilibrium dynamics in complex systems, relevant to open quantum systems modeling.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: The mid-rapidity window in the longitudinal direction z at freezeout time. The window (box in the figure) is centered around z0 width ∆. The vertical direction denotes the transverse coordinate that is integrated out, resulting an effective spatial 1D description. The white arrows represent the fluid/particle velocities in Bjorken flow.

Figure 2: Schematic illustration of the background trajectories used in this work in the (T, µ) plane of QCD phase diagram. We consider constant-µ trajectories (vertical lines) with a prescribed cooling history T(t). The sequence of colored markers indicates representative times along each trajectory (earlier →later as T decreases). The progressive decreasing color intensity of the marker color encodes the decrease of temperature, while the increasing marker size is a purely schematic indication of the growth of an effective macroscopic domain size as the system evolves. The shaded regions label the phases of the schematic QCD phase diagram, and the lightly shaded band indicates the...

Figure 3: Time-dependent thermodynamic and transport inputs along the background evolution at fixed baryon chemical potential. Shown are the susceptibilities χ2(t), χ3(t), χ4(t) (from the 3D Ising mapping evaluated along fixed µ) and the diffusion coefficient γ(t) used in the evolution equations of stochastic MC diffusion. Results are displayed for two representative constant-µ trajectories, µ = µfar ≡0.100 GeV (red) and µ = µnear ≡0.366 GeV (blue). The pronounced non-monotonic structures in χn(t) for the larger µ reflect passing close to the critical region (with µc = 0.4 GeV in our parameter choice) and induce a corresponding time dependence of γ(t) through the adopted parametrization.

Figure 4: Time evolution of the normalized two-point Wigner function f W2(q, t) along the constant- µ trajectory at µ = µfar = 0.100 GeV, far from the critical region. Colored solid curves show the MC evolution with finite current-relaxation time, while colored dashed curves show the Fick- ian diffusion limit, τ = 0, for the same values of q. The black curve denotes the instantaneous local-equilibrium value W eq 2 (t), determined by the time-dependent susceptibility along the same background trajectory; it serves as a common reference for both the MC and Fickian evolutions. The left and right columns correspond to τ = 0.2 fm and τ = 1.2 fm, respectively. The upper panels show representative...

Figure 5: Same as Fig. 4, but for the constant-µ trajectory at µ = µnear = 0.366 GeV, which passes close to the critical region. Compared with Fig. 4, the near-critical trajectory makes the finite-τ effects more visible, especially for τ = τlarge = 1.2 fm, where the MC curves show both an additional delay and a mild reshaping of the relaxation profile relative to the Fickian baseline.
